attributes.stem:
A 51-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with progressively worsening headache over three months. The pain is diffuse but worse on the right side, exacerbated by coughing and lifting. He reports associated nausea, vomiting, seizures, and left arm weakness. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He worked in synthetic rubber manufacturing for 30 years.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 145/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Neurological examination reveals power of 3/5 in the left upper limb with normal tone and reflexes. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable. A CT scan of the head is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation and imaging findings are most consistent with glioblastoma. It is the most common malignant glial tumour. The relatively short history (three months) of progressive neurological deficit with features of raised intracranial pressure is typical. Patients may also present with slowly progressive neurologic deficit (e.g. motor weakness, headache, cognitive impairment and seizures) and generalised manifestations of increased intracranial pressure such as headaches, nausea and vomiting. The CT demonstrates characteristic findings of an irregular ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis and significant mass effect crossing the midline. The patient's occupational history of synthetic rubber manufacturing is a recognised risk factor.

Meningioma is a tumour of the meninges and typically appears as a well-circumscribed, homogeneously enhancing extra-axial mass with a dural tail sign. They generally have a much longer clinical course and are more common in women. Symptoms include headache, double or blurred vision, hearing disability, tinnitus, loss of memory, weakness in arms and legs, loss of smell, seizures, numbness or speech problems. On imaging it is seen to be arising from meninges. The imaging appearance and rapid progression in this case would be unusual for meningioma.

Metastatic disease does not typically present with singular lesions but rather with multiple lesions from a known primary malignancy (e.g., lung, breast, colon, kidney and melanoma) which spreads to the brain. Patients may present with a headache, sometimes with vomiting or nausea, mental changes (e.g. as increasing memory problems), seizures weakness or unilateral numbness. While solitary metastases can occur, they tend to be multiple and well-circumscribed with more uniform enhancement. The absence of a known primary cancer makes this less likely.

Astrocytoma is a rare WHO grade III tumour arising from star-shaped brain cells called astrocytes, and typically shows less necrosis and less prominent enhancement than glioblastoma. Signs and symptoms vary depending on the location and size of the tumour and may include headaches, drowsiness, vomiting, and changes in personality or mental status, seizures, weakness of the limbs, tingling or burning sensations, balance problems, difficulties communicating, vision problems, and back pain. While it can present similarly, the imaging findings are more consistent with glioblastoma.

Pseudotumor cerebri (idiopathic intracranial hypertension) presents with symptoms of raised intracranial pressure but has normal neuroimaging. The most common symptoms of pseudotumour cerebri include recurrent headaches, visual field loss, and pulsatile tinnitus. Pseudotumour cerebri can be diagnosed with a normal MRI of the brain. and it is a diagnosis of exclusion. The presence of a space-occupying lesion excludes this diagnosis.
learningPoints: Glioblastoma typically presents with a short history (3 months) of progressive neurological deficits and features of raised intracranial pressure.
CT findings characteristic of glioblastoma include irregular ring enhancement with central necrosis and significant mass effect.
Occupational exposure to synthetic rubber manufacturing is a risk factor for glioblastoma.
references: Daniels LB, Shaya M, Nordberg ML, Shorter CD, Fowler M, Nanda A. Glioblastoma multiforme in two non-nuclear family members. J La State Med Soc. 2007; 159:215–22.
Winters JL, Wilson D, Davis DG. Congenital glioblastoma multiforme: a report of three casus and a review of the literature. J Neurol Sci. 2001; 1:13–9.

Torp SH, Solheim O, Skjulsvik AJ. The WHO 2021 Classification of Central Nervous System tumours: a practical update on what neurosurgeons need to know-a minireview. Acta Neurochir (Wien). 2022 Sep;164(9):2453-2464. doi: 10.1007/s00701-022-05301-y. Epub 2022 Jul 26. PMID: 35879477; PMCID: PMC9427889.
